message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Purpose</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To study a primarily Hispanic population of adults younger than 65 to determine if COVID-19 patients with a concurrent delirium diagnosis had worse clinical outcomes in terms of hospital stay, ventilation and mortality, than those without a delirium diagnosis.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    After approval by the appropriate Institutional Review Board, a retrospective cohort study was performed looking at demographics, vital statistics, and clinical outcomes of patients aged 18-65 admitted to a hospital in the United States – Mexico border region with COVID-19 between March 1 and June 30, 2020. Data were analyzed using Fisher’s exact test, or an unpaired
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    -test where appropriate, and a univariate analysis was performed to establish relative risk. Confidence intervals were set at 95% and
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    values ≤0.05 were considered significant.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    133 patients with confirmed COVID-19 diagnoses (58% men, 92% Hispanic) were included. Mean age was 50.5 with a standard deviation of 11.7 years (range 20-65 years). The prevalence of delirium was 6%. Fifty percent of delirium patients died during hospitalization compared to 15% of patients without delirium. Patients with delirium were found to spend more days hospitalized, in the intensive care unit, and intubated than their counterparts without delirium. Delirium was associated with increased risk of being placed on mechanical ventilation (RR 3.91, 95% CI 1.46—10.41,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    value 0.006).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Delirium was associated with worse COVID-19 outcomes independent of age. COVID-19 patients need to be actively assessed for signs of delirium and appropriate precautionary measures should be implemented. Proper documentation of delirium is key to continue learning about the incidence of delirium in COVID-19 patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Delirium in a Young Predominantly Hispanic Population with COVID-19

reference.unstructured: World Health Organization. WHO Director-General’s opening remarks at the media briefing on COVID-19. Published March 11, 2020.

reference.article-title: Unrecognized Delirium is Prevalent among Older Patients Admitted to General Medical Wards and Lead to Higher Mortality Rate

reference.unstructured: U.S. Census Bureau. El Paso CCD, El Paso County, Texas. 2020. Washington, D.C.: U.S. Census Bureau; 2021. https://data.census.gov/cedsci/profile?g=0600000US4814191215
